title: Lambda Functions
description: Small anonymous functions defined right where they're needed — the capture clause, and why STL algorithms rely on them constantly.
objectives: Understand a lambda as a function with no separate name, defined inline
Write a basic lambda and call it
Use the capture clause to access surrounding variables
Pass a lambda directly to an STL algorithm

sections.text: 1. The Problem: A Tiny One-Off Function
Recall Functions: writing a full named function, with a declaration and definition, is straightforward — but slightly heavyweight for tiny logic that's only ever used in one specific spot.
A lambda is an anonymous function, defined directly at the point it's needed, without requiring a separate name or declaration.

sections.title: Breaking it down
points: [] — the capture clause (empty here — nothing from outside is used)
(int n) — the parameter list, exactly like an ordinary function
{ return n % 2 == 0; } — the body, exactly like an ordinary function

sections.text: auto (previewed here, its own topic next) lets us store the lambda in a variable and call it like any function — isEven(4). Lambdas don't have to be stored, though; they're most often written directly inline, as we'll see next.

rows: Captures nothing from the surrounding scope
Captures x by value (a copy) — recall Pass by Value
Captures x by reference — recall Pass by Reference
Captures everything used from the surrounding scope, by value
Captures everything used from the surrounding scope, by reference

sections.text: Without capturing threshold in some form, the lambda [](int n) { return n > threshold; } wouldn't compile — a lambda can only see variables from its surrounding scope that it has explicitly (or via [=]/[&]) captured.
6. Why Lambdas Matter for STL Algorithms

Recall STL Algorithms' pattern: algorithm(begin, end, ...). find_if takes a range plus a condition — and a lambda is the natural, inline way to express "whatever this specific condition is" right where find_if is called, instead of writing and naming a separate function just for this one check.

sections.title: A sticky note instead of a filed document
sections.text: Writing a full named function is like filing an official numbered document for something you'll reference by name later. A lambda is a quick sticky note, written right where it's needed, for a thought that's only relevant in that one spot — no filing, no naming, just handed directly to whoever needs it.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting the capture clause when a lambda needs an outside variable — [](int n) { return n > threshold; } won't compile without capturing threshold.
Capturing by value ([x]) when a live, up-to-date reference was actually needed, or vice versa — the choice has the same implications as Pass by Value vs Pass by Reference.
Overusing [=] or [&] out of convenience when only one or two specific variables are actually needed — less clear about what the lambda actually depends on.
Writing an unnecessary named function for logic that's only ever used once in an STL algorithm call — a lambda is usually more direct.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a lambda function?
questions.answer: An anonymous function defined directly at the point it's needed, without a separate name or declaration.

questions.question: What does the capture clause [] do in a lambda?
questions.answer: It specifies which variables from the surrounding scope the lambda can access, and whether by value (a copy) or by reference.

questions.question: Why are lambdas commonly used with STL algorithms like find_if?
questions.answer: They let a specific, one-off condition be written directly inline at the call site, instead of requiring a separate named function just for that single use.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A lambda is an anonymous function, written directly where it's needed: [](int n) { return n % 2 == 0; }. The capture clause, [], controls which surrounding variables the lambda can use — [x] captures by value, [&x] by reference, mirroring the same Pass by Value vs Pass by Reference distinction seen earlier. Lambdas are especially common with STL algorithms like find_if(begin, end, condition), letting a one-off condition be expressed inline rather than requiring a separately named function for something used in only one place.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Lambda Functions
sections.text: Lambda = a sticky note function, no filing needed. [] = what it's allowed to see from outside. [x] copies, [&x] refers to the real thing.
